claims.proposition: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for extortion.
claims.cited_case: Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.)
quoted_text: Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion
claims.brief_sentence: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for “extortion.” See Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.) (affirming dismissal; “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion”), citing First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.), and Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.).

claims.proposition: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for extortion.
claims.cited_case: First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.)
claims.brief_sentence: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for “extortion.” See Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.) (affirming dismissal; “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion”), citing First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.), and Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.).

claims.proposition: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for extortion.
claims.cited_case: Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.)
claims.brief_sentence: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for “extortion.” See Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.) (affirming dismissal; “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion”), citing First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.), and Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.).

claims.proposition: A trade-secret plaintiff must take reasonable measures to maintain the secrecy of the claimed trade secret.
claims.cited_case: In re Protech Indus., 51 F.4th 714, 720-22 (6th Cir. 2022)
claims.brief_sentence: Further Kettering fails to identify any trade secret with particularity or reasonable secrecy measures. See 18 U.S.C. § 1839(5); In re Protech Indus., 51 F.4th 714, 720-22 (6th Cir. 2022) (reasonable measures); Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 919-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011) (particularity and OUTSA preemption).

claims.proposition: A trade secret must be identified with particularity, and overlapping common-law claims are preempted by the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
claims.cited_case: Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 919-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011)
claims.brief_sentence: Further Kettering fails to identify any trade secret with particularity or reasonable secrecy measures. See 18 U.S.C. § 1839(5); In re Protech Indus., 51 F.4th 714, 720-22 (6th Cir. 2022) (reasonable measures); Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 919-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011) (particularity and OUTSA preemption).

claims.proposition: Accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, foreclosing Kettering's CFAA claim.
claims.cited_case: Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021)
claims.brief_sentence: The alleged Computer Fraud and Abuse Act claim is foreclosed by Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021) and Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020), because Defendant Collier had accessed the information with valid credentials and at Kettering's direction during a system outage.

claims.proposition: Accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, foreclosing Kettering's CFAA claim.
claims.cited_case: Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020)
claims.brief_sentence: The alleged Computer Fraud and Abuse Act claim is foreclosed by Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021) and Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020), because Defendant Collier had accessed the information with valid credentials and at Kettering's direction during a system outage.

claims.proposition: HIPAA provides no private right of action.
claims.cited_case: Wilson v. Collins, 517 F.3d 421, 429 (6th Cir. 2008)
claims.brief_sentence: HIPAA provides no private right of action, and the materials at issue are de-identified by subject codes, not “PHI.” See Wilson v. Collins, 517 F.3d 421, 429 (6th Cir. 2008); 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(2).

claims.proposition: Overlapping common-law torts are displaced by the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
claims.cited_case: Stolle Mach. Co. v. RAM Precision Indus., 605 F. App'x 473, 484 (6th Cir. 2015)
claims.brief_sentence: Overlapping torts are displaced by the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act. See Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Stolle Mach. Co. v. RAM Precision Indus., 605 F. App'x 473, 484 (6th Cir. 2015).

claims.proposition: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.
claims.cited_case: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009)
claims.brief_sentence: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face. Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009) (internal quotation marks omitted); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007).

claims.proposition: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.
claims.cited_case: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007)
claims.brief_sentence: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face. Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009) (internal quotation marks omitted); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007).

claims.proposition: Courts disregard legal conclusions couched as factual allegations.
claims.cited_case: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009)
claims.brief_sentence: Courts disregard legal conclusions couched as factual allegations. Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678.

claims.proposition: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion.
claims.cited_case: Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.)
claims.brief_sentence: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion. Simpson, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15; Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6; Heskett, 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26.

claims.proposition: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion.
claims.cited_case: First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.)
claims.brief_sentence: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion. Simpson, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15; Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6; Heskett, 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26.

claims.proposition: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion.
claims.cited_case: Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.)
claims.brief_sentence: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion. Simpson, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15; Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6; Heskett, 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26.

claims.proposition: Communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged.
claims.brief_sentence: To the extent Kettering reframes its grievance as defamation or abuse-of-process, communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged. Hecht v. Levin, 66 Ohio St. 3d 458, 460-61, 613 N.E.2d 585 (1993); Surace v. Wilier, 25 Ohio St. 3d 229, 232-33, 495 N.E.2d 939 (1986).

claims.proposition: Communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged.
claims.cited_case: Surace v. Wilier, 25 Ohio St. 3d 229, 232-33, 495 N.E.2d 939 (1986)
claims.brief_sentence: To the extent Kettering reframes its grievance as defamation or abuse-of-process, communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged. Hecht v. Levin, 66 Ohio St. 3d 458, 460-61, 613 N.E.2d 585 (1993); Surace v. Wilier, 25 Ohio St. 3d 229, 232-33, 495 N.E.2d 939 (1986).

claims.proposition: A wrongful threat to file false criminal charges constitutes extortion (the wrongful-threat element of civil extortion).
claims.cited_for: threat to file false criminal charges constituted extortion
claims.cited_case: State v. Carter, 72 Ohio App.3d 553 (2d Dist. 1991)
claims.brief_sentence: Wrongful Threat — an unlawful threat unrelated to any valid legal claim. State v. Carter, 72 Ohio App.3d 553 (2d Dist. 1991) – threat to file false criminal charges constituted extortion.

claims.proposition: The intent element of extortion is satisfied only where threats are unrelated to lawful rights.
claims.cited_for: intent element satisfied only where threats are unrelated to lawful rights
claims.cited_case: State v. Milam, 2022-Ohio-3965 (10th Dist.)
claims.brief_sentence: Intent to Obtain Value or Coerce — purpose to obtain money, property, or concessions through improper means. State v. Milam, 2022-Ohio-3965 (10th Dist.) – intent element satisfied only where threats are unrelated to lawful rights.

claims.proposition: Demands made without a good-faith basis are unprotected (the no-legitimate-claim element of extortion).
claims.cited_case: Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co., 72 Ohio St.3d 415, 419 (1995)
claims.brief_sentence: No Legitimate Claim or Privilege — demands made without a good-faith basis are unprotected. Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co., 72 Ohio St.3d 415, 419 (1995).

claims.proposition: Mere hard bargaining is not extortion where claims are legitimate and the conduct threatened is lawful (the damages element of extortion).
claims.cited_case: United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d 1198, 1205 (11th Cir. 2002)
claims.brief_sentence: Mere “hard bargaining,” even when the consequences are severe, is not extortion where claims are legitimate and conduct threatened is lawful. See United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d 1198, 1205 (11th Cir. 2002).

claims.proposition: Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat.
claims.cited_case: United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999)
quoted_text: Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat.
claims.brief_sentence: “Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat.” United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999). Thus, advising Kettering of Ms. Collier's statutory obligations cannot constitute extortion.

claims.proposition: Seeking a monetary settlement tied directly to legal claims does not become extortion merely because regulatory reporting is also contemplated.
claims.cited_case: Flatley v. Mauro, 39 Cal.4th 299, 331 (2006)
quoted_text: Seeking a monetary settlement tied directly to legal claims does not become extortion merely because regulatory reporting is also contemplated.
claims.brief_sentence: “Seeking a monetary settlement tied directly to legal claims does not become extortion merely because regulatory reporting is also contemplated.” — Flatley v. Mauro, 39 Cal.4th 299, 331 (2006).

claims.proposition: Where claims are based on enforceable legal rights, settlement demands are privileged.
claims.cited_case: Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co., 72 Ohio St.3d 415, 419 (1995)
quoted_text: Where claims are based on enforceable legal rights, settlement demands are privileged.
claims.brief_sentence: “Where claims are based on enforceable legal rights, settlement demands are privileged.” — Kenty, 72 Ohio St.3d at 419.

claims.proposition: Advising a party of possible consequences from lawful reporting cannot form the basis of an extortion claim.
claims.cited_case: United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d 1198, 1205 (11th Cir. 2002)
quoted_text: Advising of possible consequences from lawful reporting cannot form the basis of an extortion claim.
claims.brief_sentence: “Advising of possible consequences from lawful reporting cannot form the basis of an extortion claim.” — United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d at 1205.

claims.proposition: Ohio's public policy protections shield reporting of misconduct to regulators and sponsors as protected conduct.
claims.cited_case: Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc., 78 Ohio St.3d 134, 150 (1997)
claims.brief_sentence: Reporting misconduct to regulators and sponsors is protected conduct: Whistleblower Protection Act, 5 U.S.C. § 2302; False Claims Act retaliation provisions, 31 U.S.C. § 3730(h); Ohio's public policy protections (Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc., 78 Ohio St.3d 134, 150 (1997)).

claims.proposition: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion.
claims.cited_case: Flatley v. Mauro, 39 Cal.4th 299, 330 (2006)
claims.brief_sentence: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion. Flatley, 39 Cal.4th at 330; Jackson, 180 F.3d at 70.

claims.proposition: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion.
claims.cited_case: United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999)
claims.brief_sentence: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion. Flatley, 39 Cal.4th at 330; Jackson, 180 F.3d at 70.

claims.proposition: A complaint that identifies only broad categories of information does not satisfy the trade-secret particularity requirement.
claims.cited_case: Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 919-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011)
claims.brief_sentence: The Complaint identifies only broad categories of information “clinical data” and “research processes”, which do not satisfy the particularity requirement. Office Depot, 821 F. Supp. 2d at 919-23.

claims.proposition: A trade-secret plaintiff must plead reasonable measures to maintain secrecy.
claims.cited_case: In re Protech Indus., 51 F.4th 714, 720-22 (6th Cir. 2022)
claims.brief_sentence: The Complaint also fails to plead reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. In re Protech, 51 F.4th at 720-22.

claims.proposition: The Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act displaces overlapping common-law tort claims based on the same nucleus of facts.
claims.cited_case: Stolle Mach. Co. v. RAM Precision Indus., 605 F. App'x 473, 484 (6th Cir. 2015)
claims.brief_sentence: Moreover, to the extent Kettering asserts overlapping torts based on the same nucleus of facts, the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act displaces those claims—Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Stolle Mach., 605 F. App'x at 484.

claims.proposition: The Supreme Court construes “exceeds authorized access” narrowly to cover obtaining information from areas of a computer to which a person lacks access privileges, not misuse of information obtained with authorized credentials.
claims.cited_case: Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021)
claims.brief_sentence: The Supreme Court construes “exceeds authorized access” narrowly to cover obtaining information from particular areas of a computer—such as files, folders, or databases—to which a person lacks access privileges, not misuse of information obtained with authorized credentials. Van Buren, 593 U.S. at 380-86.

claims.proposition: The Sixth Circuit applies the same narrow rule that accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the CFAA.
claims.cited_case: Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020)
claims.brief_sentence: The Sixth Circuit applies the same rule. Royal Truck, 974 F.3d at 758-61.

claims.proposition: HIPAA does not create a private right of action.
claims.cited_case: Wilson v. Collins, 517 F.3d 421, 429 (6th Cir. 2008)
claims.brief_sentence: HIPAA does not create a private right of action. Wilson, 517 F.3d at 429.

claims.proposition: Tortious-interference and conversion theories are preempted by OUTSA where the alleged harm stems from misappropriation.
claims.cited_case: Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 918-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011)
claims.brief_sentence: Its tortious-interference and conversion theories are preempted by OUTSA where the alleged harm stems from misappropriation. Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Office Depot, 821 F. Supp. 2d at 918-23.

claims.proposition: Any defamation-based claim arising from litigation conduct is absolutely privileged.
claims.brief_sentence: Any defamation-based claim arising from litigation conduct is absolutely privileged. Hecht, 66 Ohio St. 3d at 460-61.
